skill.name: aws-serverless
skill.description: Guidance for building production-ready AWS serverless applications with Lambda, API Gateway, DynamoDB, SQS, SNS, SAM, and CDK patterns.
skill.summary: Build AWS serverless applications with practical Lambda, API Gateway, DynamoDB, and event-driven patterns.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many high-severity patterns, but review found no malicious behavior or prompt injection in SKILL.md. Most findings are false positives caused by markdown code fences, AWS SAM terminology, a relative import, and normal configuration examples. The only residual concerns are low-risk example hardening issues such as wildcard CORS and verbose error logging.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Documentation Backticks Flagged as Commands
low_findings.description: Static analysis treated markdown fences and one JavaScript template literal as shell execution. These are documentation examples in SKILL.md, not executable skill code, and no command execution instruction is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are markdown fence delimiters or a JavaScript logging template literal. I found no evidence that the skill itself executes Ruby, shell, or any external command.

low_findings.title: False Positive: AWS SAM Text Flagged as Sensitive Windows SAM
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged SAM references as the Windows SAM database and a weak cryptographic algorithm. In context, the file describes AWS Serverless Application Model templates and deployment approaches.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text is clearly AWS serverless documentation. No evidence found of Windows credential database access or weak cryptographic operations.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Relative Import Flagged as Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The ../lib/dynamodb string is a normal Node.js relative import inside an example handler. It does not read arbitrary filesystem paths and does not use user-controlled input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The path appears inside a static require statement in documentation. No evidence found of dynamic path construction or filesystem access.

low_findings.title: Benign Environment Variable Configuration Examples
low_findings.description: The examples read TABLE_NAME from environment variables for Lambda configuration. This is a normal AWS pattern and does not expose secrets or send environment data to another system.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only environment variable shown is a DynamoDB table name. No evidence found of credential harvesting, secret dumping, or exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Benign AWS API Gateway URL Example
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL pattern is a CloudFormation output template for an AWS API Gateway endpoint. It documents deployed API output and is not an unauthorized external network request.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is generated from AWS template variables and not contacted by the skill. No evidence found of data exfiltration or suspicious external domains.

low_findings.title: Example Code Uses Permissive Defaults
low_findings.description: Some examples use wildcard CORS headers and return or log detailed error information. This is not malicious, but production users should restrict origins and avoid exposing sensitive error details.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples show permissive CORS and verbose error handling. These are common development defaults, but they need review before production use.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance Terms in AWS Examples
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance findings point to ordinary API and DynamoDB example fields such as id parameters and key schema entries. These lines do not inspect the host system or gather local machine information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are static documentation examples for request IDs and DynamoDB keys. No evidence found of host reconnaissance commands or local system enumeration.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Dangerous Combination Heuristic Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The heuristic combined code fences, an AWS URL template, environment variable examples, and a relative import. Manual review found documentation-only examples and no credential access, exfiltration path, or executable network behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The ingredients for the heuristic are individually benign in context. No evidence found that they combine into credential theft, obfuscation, or malicious execution.

content.value_statement: Serverless teams need reliable patterns for Lambda, APIs, data, and async events. This skill provides practical AWS examples for designing, implementing, and hardening production serverless services.
actual_capabilities: Explains Lambda handler structure for Node.js and Python examples.
Shows API Gateway integration patterns using AWS SAM templates.
Documents DynamoDB access patterns for serverless handlers.
Covers SQS batch processing with partial failure handling.
Highlights common Lambda anti-patterns and cold start concerns.
Lists deployment and runtime considerations for production serverless applications.
limitations: Provides documentation examples, not a complete deployable application.
Examples are truncated in several places and require completion before use.
Does not validate AWS account permissions, quotas, or regional availability.
Security settings need review before production deployment.

use_cases.title: Design a New Serverless API
use_cases.description: Plan Lambda handlers, API Gateway routes, and DynamoDB tables for a new backend service.

use_cases.title: Improve Event Processing Reliability
use_cases.description: Apply SQS batch processing, retry behavior, and dead-letter queue guidance to async workflows.

use_cases.title: Review Lambda Production Readiness
use_cases.description: Check serverless code for cold start risks, timeouts, dependencies, and operational sharp edges.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Lambda Handler
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for one AWS Lambda function.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the AWS serverless skill to draft a basic Lambda handler for this event shape: [paste event]. Include input parsing, error handling, and response structure.

prompt_templates.title: Plan an API Gateway Service
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate API design with Lambda and DynamoDB.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the AWS serverless skill to design an API Gateway and Lambda service for [describe resource]. Include routes, handlers, DynamoDB table needs, and deployment notes.

prompt_templates.title: Build an SQS Processing Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the AWS serverless skill to design an SQS-triggered Lambda workflow for [describe workload]. Include batch behavior, partial failures, retries, and dead-letter handling.

prompt_templates.title: Review Production Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the AWS serverless skill to review this serverless design for production readiness: [paste design]. Focus on security, scaling, cold starts, observability, and failure handling.

output_examples.input: Design a Lambda-backed API for storing items in DynamoDB.
output: A route plan with read and create operations.
Handler responsibilities for validation, database access, and API responses.
Deployment notes for table permissions and environment variables.

output_examples.input: Review an SQS worker that processes messages in batches.
output: A reliability checklist covering retries, partial failures, and dead-letter queues.
Operational guidance for timeout, visibility timeout, and message retention settings.
Warnings about idempotency and error logging.

output_examples.input: Find serverless anti-patterns in my Lambda design.
output: A concise list of risks such as large dependencies, monolithic handlers, and VPC cold starts.
Suggested changes to reduce latency and deployment risk.
Production hardening reminders for CORS, errors, and permissions.

best_practices: Restrict IAM permissions, CORS origins, and API exposure before production deployment.
Set Lambda timeout, memory, and queue visibility values based on measured workload behavior.
Keep handlers small, initialize clients outside handlers, and design async work to be idempotent.
anti_patterns: Do not copy truncated examples directly into production systems.
Do not use wildcard CORS settings for private or authenticated APIs.
Do not combine unrelated operations into one large Lambda handler.

faq.question: Does this skill deploy AWS resources?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples that a user can adapt for AWS deployment workflows.

faq.question: Which AWS services does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers Lambda, API Gateway, DynamoDB, SQS, SNS concepts, SAM, CDK, and cold start optimization topics.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Are the examples production-ready as written?
faq.answer: No. Several examples are incomplete and need security, testing, and deployment review before use.

faq.question: Does it include security guidance?
faq.answer: It includes operational warnings, but users should add explicit IAM, CORS, logging, and secret management reviews.

faq.question: What is the main limitation?
faq.answer: The skill is a compact reference, so it does not replace full AWS documentation or architecture review.
